title: Protein expression using E. coli strain BL21DE3
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"bhap9","text":"In this protocol will be explained how to express proteins using E. coli strain BL21DE3 using a plasmid with a T7 promoter. The promoter of T7 polymerase from E. coli BL21DE3 has to be induced with IPTG.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":65,"length":7},{"style":"italic","offset":159,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9u2kk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3tqm9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Prepare an overnight culture of the bacterium encoding for the protein you want to express.
Prepare an Erlenmeyer flask containing LB medium and the right antibiotics
Inoculate the Erlenmeyer flask 1:100 from your overnight culture and grow the bacteria at 37°C and 120 rpm.
Grow the culture to an OD600 of 0.6. This will usually take 2-3 hours.
Take 1 ml of pre-induction sample and cold shock the culture for 30 minutes on ice. Keep the pre-induction sample in the fridge or freezer.

Add IPTG to a final maximum concentration of 1μM. IPTG is used because E. coli BL21DE3 has its T7 polymerase gene under an IPTG sensitive promoter and we have used plasmids with a T7 promoter in our protein expression experiments
Grow the culture overnight (for 18hours) at 20°C and 120 rpm.
Measure the OD600of a 10x diluted post-induction sample. Calculate the “real” optical density by multiplying this number by 10.
Take 1 ml of post-induction sample and spin this, together with the pre-induction sample down for 10 minutes at 4700 g, using a tabletop centrifuge.

Resuspend the pellet of the pre-induction sample in 100μl of MQ. To correct for the difference in OD600 when you load the samples on a protein gel, resuspend the pellet of the post-induction sample in 100μl x ("real"OD600 post−induction sample)/(OD600pre−induction sample)
Prepare the samples to be loaded on a protein gel
Run both the pre-induction and post-induction sample on a protein gel
creator: Robert Hooftman
